On the Duties of the Clergy.
Chapter XL. Courage in war was not wanting in our forefathers, as is shown by the example of the men of old, especially by the glorious deed of Eleazar.

title: Chapter XL. Courage in war was not wanting in our forefathers, as is shown by the example of the men of old, especially by the glorious deed of Eleazar.

source.title: NPNF2-10. Ambrose: Selected Works and Letters

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But perhaps renown in war keeps some so bound to itself as to make them think that fortitude is to be found in battle alone, and that therefore I had gone aside to speak of these things, because that was wanting in us.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But how brave was Joshua the son of Nun, who in one battle laid low five kings together with their people!

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Again, when he fought against the Gibeonites and feared that night might stop him from gaining the victory, he called out with deep faith and high spirit: “Let the sun stand still;” and it stood still until the victory was complete.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Gideon with three hundred men gained a triumph over a great nation and a cruel foe.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Jonathan when a young man showed great courage in battle, and what shall I say about the Maccabees?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: First, I will speak of the people of our fathers.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: They were ready to fight for the temple of God and for their rights, and when attacked on the Sabbath day by the craft of the enemy, willingly allowed wounds to be inflicted on their unprotected bodies, rather than to join in the fight, so that they might not defile the Sabbath.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: They all gladly gave themselves up to death.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But the Maccabees thinking that then all the nation would perish, on the Sabbath also, when they were challenged to fight, took vengeance for the death of their innocent brethren.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And afterwards when he had been roused by this to fresh exertions, King Antiochus, having begun the war afresh under the leadership of his generals Lysias, Nicanor, and Georgias, was so utterly crushed, together with his Eastern and Assyrian forces, that he left 48,000 lying on the battle-field, slain by an army of but 3,000 men.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Mark the courage of the leader, Judas Maccabæus, as exemplified in the character of one of his soldiers.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Eleazar, meeting with an elephant higher than all the rest, and with all the royal trappings upon it, and thinking that the king was on it, ran hastily and threw himself into the midst of the legion; and, casting away his shield, with both hands he slew those opposed to him until he reached the beast.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Then he got beneath it, thrust in his sword and slew it.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But the beast in falling crushed Eleazar and so killed him.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What courage of mind was his then, first, in that he feared not death, next because, when surrounded by enemies, he was carried by it into the thickest of his foes and penetrated the very centre!

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Then, despising death, and casting away his shield, he ran beneath the huge beast, wounded it with both his hands, and let it fall upon him.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: He ran beneath it so as to give a more deadly blow.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Enclosed by its fall, rather than crushed, he was buried in his own triumph.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Nor was he deceived in his intention though he was deceived by the royal ornaments.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For the enemy, startled at such an exhibition of valour, dared not rush upon this single unarmed man, held fast though he was.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: They were so terrified after the mischance of the slaughter of the beast, that they considered themselves altogether unequal to the valour of one.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Nay, King Antiochus, son of Lysias, terrified at the fortitude of one, asked for peace.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: He had come to the war with 120,000 armed men and with 32 elephants, which glittered and gleamed with the sheen of arms like a line of burning lamps, as the sun rose upon them, marching along one by one, like very mountains for size.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Thus Eleazar left peace as the heir of his courage.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: These are the signs of triumphs.
